concepts.title: Progressions
concepts.conceptBody:
Progression & Series 

Introduction 

A sequence is a function whose domain is the set $\text{N}$ of natural numbers.

For example 4, 1, 3 is a sequence.

whereas series is an expression achieved by adding or subtracting the terms in sequence.

i.e. if ${{a}_{1}},{{a}_{2}},{{a}_{3}},\ldots {{a}_{n}}$ is a sequence, then the expression ${{a}_{1}}+{{a}_{2}}+{{a}_{3}}\ldots +{{a}_{n}}$ is a series.

A series is finite or infinite according as the number of terms in the corresponding sequence is finite or infinite.

It is not necessary that the terms of a sequence always follow a certain pattern or they are described by some formula or its ${{r}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term.

Progression are sequences whose terms follow certain patterns.

concepts.title: Explaination of Arithmetic Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Arithmetic Progression 

An arithmetic progression is a sequence of numbers where each new term after the first is formed by adding a fixed amount called the common difference to the previous term in the sequence. For example the sequence $3,5,7,9,11\ldots $ is an arithmetic progression. Note that having chosen the first term to be 3, each new term is found by adding 2 to the previous term, so the common difference is 2 .

The common difference can be negative : for example the sequence $2,-1,-4,-7,\ldots $ is an arithmetic progression with first term 2 and common difference -3 . In general we can write an arithmetic progression as follows :

arithmetic progression : $\text{a},\text{a}+\text{d},\text{a}+2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d},\text{a}+3\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d},\ldots $

where the first term is a and the common difference is d. Some important results concerning arithmetic progressions (A.P.) now follow :

$\text{ The } {{\text{n}}^{\text{th } }} \text{ term } \text{ of } \text{ an } \text{ a}\text{.p}\text{. } \text{ is } \text{ given } \text{ by } \text{ : } \text{ a } +\left( n-1 \right)d$

and in general

${{a}_{n}}={{a}_{m}}+\left( n-m \right)d$

A finite portion of an arithmetic progression is called a finite arithmetic progression and sometimes just called an arithmetic progression.

${{t}_{n}}-{{t}_{n-1}} \text{ is } \text{ constant } \text{ for } \text{ all } n\in N\text{. } \text{ This } \text{ constant } d={{t}_{n}}-{{t}_{n-1}}$

${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term from the end 

If ' $a$ ' is the first term of an AP & ' $d$ ' is the common difference, then the ${{\text{m}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term from end is ${{(n-m+1)}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term from the beginning. (where $n$ is the no. of terms in AP).

concepts.title: Solved Problems based on the definition of Arithmetic Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved examples

Q) Show that the sequence defined by ${{a}_{n}}=2{{n}^{2}}+1$ is not an A.P. 

Solution : 

We have ${{a}_{n}}=2{{n}^{2}}+1$

replacing $\text{n}$ by $\text{n}+1$

${{\text{a}}_{\text{n}+1}}=2{{(\text{n}+1)}^{2}}+1$

According to the definition of AP the difference of two consecutive terms is always a constant.

So, if the sequence has to be in AP then the difference ${{a}_{n+1}}-{{a}_{n}}$ should be a constant.

Now, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{a}_{n+1}}-{{a}_{n}}=\left[ 2{{(n+1)}^{2}}+1 \right]-\left( 2{{n}^{2}}+1 \right)$

$=\left( 2\left( {{\text{n}}^{2}}+2\text{n}+1 \right)+1 \right)-\left( 2{{\text{n}}^{2}}+1 \right)$

$=4\text{n}+2$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( {{a}_{n+1}}-{{a}_{n}} \right)$ is not a constant, not independent of $n$ so the given sequence is not an AP.

Q) Find the number of terms common to the two APs : 3, 7, 11, ... 407 and 2, 9, 16 ..., 709 

Solution : 

Let the no. of terms in two AP's be $m$ and $n$.

Then $n$th term of ${{\text{I}}^{\text{st}}}\text{AP}=407\Rightarrow \text{m}=3+\left( \text{m}-1 \right)4$

${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }}}\text{ term }\text{ of }{{2}^{\text{nd }}}\text{AP}=709\Rightarrow \text{n}=2+\left( \text{n}-1 \right)7$

$\Rightarrow \text{ m}=102$

& $\text{n}=102$

So each AP consists of 102 terms.

Let ${{p}^{\text{th }}}$ term of first AP be identical to ${{q}^{\text{th }}}$ term of second AP.

Then $3+(p-1)=2+(q-1) 7$

$\Rightarrow 4 p-1=7 q-5$

$\Rightarrow 4 p+4=7 a$

$\Rightarrow 4(p+1)=7 q$

$\Rightarrow \frac{p+1}{7}=\frac{q}{4}=K$

$\Rightarrow \text{p}=7\text{K}-1\text{ q}=4\text{K}$

$\Rightarrow \text{p}=7\text{ K}-1\And \text{q}=4\text{ K}$

and we know that $\text{p}$ has to be less than $102\And \text{q}$ also has to be less than 102

Since the total no. of terms are 102

For $\mathrm{p}=7 \mathrm{K}-1 \leq 102$

we get $7 \mathrm{K} \leq 103$

$\mathrm{K} \leq \frac{103}{7} \Rightarrow \mathrm{K} \leq 14 \frac{5}{7}$ (1)

From $q=4K\le 102$

$\Rightarrow \text{K}\le 25\frac{1}{2}$ (2)

from (i) & (ii) we get

$\text{K}\le 14\Rightarrow \text{K}=1,2,3,\ldots 14$

concepts.title: Sum of a Finite Arithmetic Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Sum of a finite arithmetic progression 

The sum of the numbers of a finite arithmetic progression is called an arithmetic series.

Express the arithmetic series in two different ways :

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}={{\text{a}}_{1}}+\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}+\text{d} \right)+\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{2}_{\text{d}}} \right)+\ldots +\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}+\left( \text{n}-2 \right)\text{d} \right)+\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}+\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\text{d} \right) \\ {} & {{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}-\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\text{d} \right)+\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}-\left( \text{n}-2 \right)\text{d} \right)+\ldots +\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}-2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d} \right)+\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}-\text{d} \right)+{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}. \\ \end{array}$

Add both sides of the two equations. All terms involving d cancel, and so we're left with :

$2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\text{n}\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}} \right)\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Rearranging and remembering that ${{a}_{n}}={{a}_{1}}+\left( n-1 \right)d$, we get

${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\frac{\text{n}\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}} \right)}{2}=\frac{\text{n}\left[ 2{{\text{a}}_{1}}+\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\text{d} \right]}{2}$

Gaussian Trick 

Let us write the sum of the natural numbers up to $\text{n}$ in two ways as :

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=1+2+3+\ldots +\left( \text{n}-2 \right)+\left( \text{n}-1 \right)+\text{n} \\ {} & \text{Sn}=\text{n}+\left( \text{n}-1 \right)+\left( \text{n}-2 \right)+\ldots +3+2+1 \\ \end{array}$

If we add these two series we get :

$2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\left( \text{n}+1 \right)+\left( \text{n}+1 \right)+\ldots +\left( \text{n}+1 \right)$

There are $n$ of these $\left( n+1 \right)$ 's, so

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\text{n}\left( \text{n}+1 \right) \\ {} & {{\mathbf{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\text{n}\left( \text{n}+1 \right)/2 \\ \end{array}$

The sum of the natural numbers from 1 to $\text{n}$ is therefore half the product of the first term plus the last one multiplied by the number of terms.

concepts.title: Properties on Arithmetic Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
More Properties of A.P. 

(1) If a fixed number is added (subtracted) to each term of a given AP then resulting sequence is also AP with same common difference as given AP.

${{t}_{n}}=a+\left( n-1 \right)d$, now let $g$ be subtracted from each term.

${{t}_{n}}=\left( a-g \right)+\left( n-1 \right)d$. Therefore only the first term of AP has changed.

(2) If each term of an AP is multiplied (or divided) by fixed constant then resulting sequence is also an AP with common difference multiplied (or divided) by same constant.

(3) Sum and difference of corresponding terms of two AP's will form an AP

Let first AP be ${{a}_{1}},{{a}_{1}}+{{d}_{1}},{{a}_{1}}+2{{d}_{1}},\ldots \ldots .,{{a}_{1}}+\left( n-1 \right){{d}_{1}}$

And second AP be ${{\text{a}}_{2}},{{\text{a}}_{2}}+{{\text{d}}_{2}},{{\text{a}}_{2}}+2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{d}}_{2}},\ldots \ldots \ldots ,{{\text{a}}_{2}}+\left( \text{n}-1 \right){{\text{d}}_{2}}$

So, ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}={{\text{t}}_{1\text{r}}}+{{\text{t}}_{2\text{r}}}$

$\begin{array}{l} {} & ~=\left( {{a}_{1}}+\left( r-1 \right){{d}_{1}}+\left( {{a}_{2}}+\left( r-1 \right){{d}_{2}} \right) \right. \\ {} & ~=\left( {{a}_{1}}+{{a}_{2}} \right)+\left( r-1 \right)\left( {{d}_{1}}+{{d}_{2}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

So, resulting sequence is A.P. with first term ${{a}_{1}}+{{a}_{2}}$ and common difference ${{d}_{1}}+{{d}_{2}}$.

(4) If we want to pick terms of an AP then convenient way of doing that is :

For three term's in AP we choose a - d, a, a $+d$.

For four terms in AP we choose $a-3d,a-d,a+d,a+3d$

Note : 

  • for odd no. of terms the middle term is a & common difference is $\text{d}$.
  • for even no. of terms the middle term is $a-d~\And~ a+d$ whereas common difference is $2d$.

(5) In a finite AP the sum of terms equidistant from the beginning and end is always same & equal to sum of first & last term i.e.,

${{\text{a}}_{\text{k}}}+{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}-\left( \text{k}-1 \right)}}={{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}$

for $\text{k}\in 1,2,\ldots \text{n}-1$

(6) ${{\text{t}}_{\text{n}}}={{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}-{{\text{S}}_{\text{n}-1}}$

(7) If $\text{a},\text{b},\text{c}$ are in $\text{AP}\Rightarrow 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}=\text{a}+\text{c}$ or $\text{b}-\text{a}=\text{c}-\text{b}$.

(8) A sequence is in AP.

  • if its ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }}}$ term is a linear expression in $n$ i.e. ${{a}_{n}}=An+B$ (where A & B are constant) $\And \text{ A}$ is the common difference and
  • if sum of its $\text{n}$ terms is of form $\text{A}{{\text{n}}^{2}}+\text{Bn}$, in such cases common difference is $2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}$.

concepts.title: Determining Finite Sum of Arithmetic Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Determining Finite Sum of Arithmetic Progression

Q) Find the number of terms in the series $20,10\frac{1}{2},18\frac{2}{2}\ldots ...$. of which the sum is 300. 

Solution : 

Here we observe that $\text{a}=20,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}=-\frac{2}{3}$, and ${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=300$.

$\mathrm{p}=7 \mathrm{~K}-1 \leq 102$

$7 \mathrm{~K} \leq 103$

$\mathrm{K} \leq \frac{103}{7} \Rightarrow \mathrm{K} \leq 14 \frac{5}{7}$

$\mathrm{a}=20, \mathrm{~d}=-\frac{2}{3}$, and $\quad \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=300$

So, $\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}\left(2 \times 20+(\mathrm{n}-1)\left(\frac{-2}{3}\right)\right)=300$

$\mathrm{n}^2-61 \mathrm{n}+900=0$

$\mathrm{n}=25$ or 36

Q) Find the sum of first 24 terms of the AP : ${{a}_{1}},{{a}_{2}},{{a}_{3}}\ldots $ if it is known that ${{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{5}}+{{\text{a}}_{10}}+{{\text{a}}_{15}}+{{\text{a}}_{20}}+{{\text{a}}_{24}}=225$

Solution : 

We know that in AP the sum of terms equidistant from beginning & end is equal & same to the sum of first & last term. i.e. ${{a}_{1}}+{{a}_{n}}={{a}_{2}}+{{a}_{n-1}}={{a}_{3}}+{{a}_{n-2}}\And $ so on.

So here ${{a}_{1}}+{{a}_{24}}={{a}_{5}}+{{a}_{20}}={{a}_{10}}+{{a}_{15}}$ (1)

Now ${{a}_{1}}+{{a}_{5}}+{{a}_{10}}+{{a}_{15}}+{{a}_{20}}+{{a}_{24}}=225$

$\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{24}} \right)+\left( {{\text{a}}_{5}}+{{\text{a}}_{20}} \right)+\left( {{\text{a}}_{10}}+{{\text{a}}_{15}} \right)=225$

$\Rightarrow 3\left( {{a}_{1}}+{{a}_{24}} \right)=225$ {from (1)}

$\Rightarrow \left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{24}} \right)=75$ (2)

$\therefore {{\text{S}}_{24}}=\frac{24}{2}\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{24}} \right)$

from (ii)

$\begin{array}{l} {{\text{S}}_{24}} =12\left( 75 \right) \\ =900 \\ \end{array}$

Q) The interior angles of a polygon are in AP. The smallest angle is 120 and the common difference is 5 . Find the number of sides of polygon. 

Solution : 

Let the polygon be of ' $n$ ' sides

So for a polygon of $\text{n}$ sides, the sum of all its interior angles is given by

${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\left( \text{n}-2 \right)180\text{ (i) }\text{ (try }\text{ to }\text{ solve }\text{ this }\text{ on }\text{ your }\text{ own)}\text{. }$

Hence the interior angles form an AP with $\text{a}=120\And \text{ d}=5$

$\Rightarrow {{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\frac{\text{n}}{2}\left[ 2\times 120+\left( \text{n}-1 \right)5 \right]$ (ii)

equating (i) & (ii)

$\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}[2 \times 120+(\mathrm{n}-1) 5]=(\mathrm{n}-2) \times 180$

$\mathrm{n}(5 \mathrm{n}+240-5)=(\mathrm{n}-2) \quad 360$

$\mathrm{n}(5 \mathrm{n}+235)=(\mathrm{n}-2) \quad 360$

$5 n^2+235 n=360 n-720$

$5 n^2-125 n+720=0$

$\mathrm{n}^2-25 \mathrm{n}+144=0$

$(\mathrm{n}=16)$ or $\mathrm{n}=9$

But the question is not over yet.

We have to check whether the values are correct or not for $\text{n}=16$.

The last angle is ${{a}_{n}}=a+\left( n-1 \right)d\Rightarrow {{a}_{16}}=120+\left( 16-1 \right)5=190$

Which is not possible (since we are looking for interior angles only)

$\therefore$ The solution is $\text{n}=9$

concepts.title: Excenter and it's properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Excentres of a triangle

This is the point of intersection of the external bisectors of the angles of a triangle.

The circle opposite to the vertex A is called the escribed circle opposite A or the circle escribed to the side $\text{BC}$. If ${{\text{I}}_{1}}$ is the point of intersection of internal bisector of $\angle \text{BAC}$ and external bisector of $\angle \text{ABC}$ and $\angle \text{ACB}$ then

$\begin{array}{l} {{\text{I}}_{1}} =\left( \frac{a{{x}_{1}}-b{{x}_{2}}-c{{x}_{3}}}{a-b-c},\frac{a{{y}_{1}}-b{{y}_{2}}-c{{y}_{3}}}{a-b-c} \right) \\ \text{ or }{{I}_{1}} =\left( \frac{-a{{x}_{1}}-b{{x}_{2}}-c{{x}_{3}}}{-a+b+c},\frac{-a{{y}_{1}}+b{{y}_{2}}+c{{y}_{3}}}{-a+b+c} \right) \\ \end{array}$

Similarly, ${{I}_{2}}=\left( \frac{a{{x}_{1}}-b{{x}_{2}}-c{{x}_{3}}}{a-b-c},\frac{a{{y}_{1}}-b{{y}_{2}}+c{{y}_{3}}}{a-b+c} \right)$

and ${{I}_{3}}=\left( \frac{a{{x}_{1}}+b{{x}_{2}}-c{{x}_{3}}}{a+b-c},\frac{a{{y}_{1}}+b{{y}_{2}}+c{{y}_{3}}}{a+b-c} \right)$

when $\left| \text{BC} \right|=\text{a},\left| \text{CA} \right|=\text{b}$ and $\left| \text{AB} \right|=\text{c}$
